title: Navigating Medicare and Health Coverage Changes for Lawfully Present Immigrants in 2026
rawContent:
Who Counts as a Non-Citizen for Medicare Purposes?

Roughly 23 million non-citizens currently live in the United States, according to the Migration Policy Institute's 2024 data. Among them, approximately 10.8 million are lawful permanent residents, many approaching or already past age 65, wondering whether Medicare is within reach. The answer depends almost entirely on how you are here, not just that you are here.

Federal health policy treats immigration categories very differently. Here is a quick breakdown:

  • Lawful permanent residents (green card holders): May qualify after meeting residency and work requirements
  • Refugees and asylees: Generally exempt from standard waiting periods
  • Cuban/Haitian entrants and certain parolees: May qualify under humanitarian exemptions
  • Visa holders (student, work, tourist): Typically not eligible for Medicare regardless of age
  • Undocumented immigrants: Excluded from Medicare entirely under current federal law

According to guidance from both the Kaiser Family Foundation (KFF) and CMS, lawfully present immigrants can qualify for Medicare under specific conditions, while undocumented individuals are generally locked out of the program. This article walks through exactly where each group stands, including what has shifted in 2026's policy environment and the concrete steps you can take right now.

Core Medicare Eligibility Rules for Non-Citizens

Two gates stand between a non-citizen and Medicare enrollment. First, your immigration status must qualify you as lawfully present, and you must have maintained continuous U.S. residence for at least five years. Second, you need the work history, generally 40 quarters (10 years) of Medicare payroll tax contributions, to receive premium-free Part A.

Once eligible, non-citizens face the same plan structure as U.S. citizens:

  • Part A (hospital insurance): Premium-free with 40+ work quarters; up to $505/month with fewer than 30 quarters (CMS, 2025)
  • Part B (medical insurance): Standard premium of $185/month in 2025–2026 for all eligible enrollees
  • Part C (Medicare Advantage): Available to non-citizens who are enrolled in Parts A and B
  • Part D (prescription drug coverage): Available alongside original Medicare or through Medicare Advantage

The Social Security Administration verifies both your work quarters and immigration status during enrollment. You will typically need your green card or other immigration documentation, Social Security number, and employment records to move forward. Juliette Cubanski, Deputy Director of the Program on Medicare Policy at KFF, puts it plainly: "Immigrant eligibility for Medicare is often misunderstood, lawful permanent residents can qualify, but the pathways depend heavily on work history, years of residency, and specific immigration status."

How Many Work Credits Do Immigrants Need for Medicare?

The 40-quarter threshold unlocks premium-free Part A. If you have 30 to 39 quarters, you pay a reduced premium, currently $278/month in 2025. Fewer than 30 quarters means the full premium of up to $505/month applies. That cost adds up quickly, making it worth exploring every avenue before assuming you owe the maximum.

Importantly, work credits from a spouse or parent can count toward your eligibility in certain situations. An immigrant senior who spent fewer years in the formal U.S. workforce, perhaps due to caregiving responsibilities or a later arrival, may still qualify for premium-free Part A through a qualifying spouse's work record. Checking this with the SSA early is worthwhile.

The 5-Year Waiting Period: What It Means and Who Is Exempt

The five-year continuous residency requirement is the single biggest barrier most lawful permanent residents face. Consider two real scenarios: a green card holder who arrived at age 60 becomes eligible for Medicare at 65, the wait aligns with the standard eligibility age. But someone who received their green card at 62 cannot enroll at 65 because the five-year clock has not yet run out. They need to wait until 67, creating a two-year gap with no Medicare access.

That gap can be financially significant, especially for those with chronic conditions who need regular care. During that window, marketplace plans or employer coverage typically serve as the only bridge.

Several immigration categories are exempt from the five-year rule entirely:

  • Refugees and asylees
  • Cuban and Haitian entrants
  • Certain trafficking victims with T visas
  • Other humanitarian-status immigrants recognized under federal law

These groups often qualify for Medicare for green card holders and Medicaid access on an accelerated timeline. Muzaffar Chishti, Senior Fellow at the Migration Policy Institute, describes the larger challenge: "The intersection of immigration status and access to public benefits like Medicare is one of the most complex areas of U. S.

policy, with real consequences for aging immigrant communities.

Medicaid vs. Medicare for Non-Citizens: Understanding the Difference

Medicare is a federal program primarily based on age (65+) or disability. Medicaid is a federal-state partnership based on income, and its eligibility rules for immigrants vary considerably from state to state. Joan Alker, Executive Director of Georgetown University's Center for Children and Families, describes it as "a patchwork of eligibility rules that can leave significant gaps in coverage, especially for those who have contributed to the system through payroll taxes."

For most lawfully present immigrants, the same five-year waiting period applies to full Medicaid as to Medicare. However, emergency Medicaid is available in most states regardless of immigration status, providing coverage for acute, life-threatening situations. It does not function as a substitute for ongoing primary or preventive care. You can learn more about how these programs compare on the Medicare vs. Medicaid overview page.

Some non-citizen seniors may qualify for dual eligibility receiving both Medicare and Medicaid simultaneously. For low-income immigrant seniors, Medicaid Savings Programs can help cover Medicare premiums, copays, and prescription costs, significantly reducing out-of-pocket expenses. The Medicaid spend-down program is one mechanism worth exploring for those who fall just above income thresholds.

State variation matters here. California, New York, and Illinois have expanded Medicaid to cover certain immigrant populations beyond the federal baseline, while many other states apply stricter federal rules. Checking your specific state's Medicaid office is always the right first step.

Emergency Medicaid: The Safety Net for Those Who Don't Qualify

Emergency Medicaid covers acute, life-threatening medical conditions, think emergency surgery, a heart attack, or a serious accident. It is available to undocumented immigrants and others excluded from full coverage programs in most states. Access varies by state, and coverage ends when the emergency resolves. This is not a long-term coverage strategy, and it should not be mistaken for comprehensive health insurance, but it does represent a meaningful safety net for those with no other options.

2026 Policy Changes Affecting Immigrant Health Coverage

The healthcare policy landscape in 2026 is shifting in ways that directly affect lawfully present immigrants. Proposed and enacted provisions under H.R. 1 include tightened Medicaid eligibility criteria and changes to how marketplace subsidies are distributed, both of which disproportionately affect immigrant families navigating the five-year gap.

Lawfully present immigrants who relied on ACA marketplace premium tax credits (PTCs) during their waiting period are among the most affected. Changes to income thresholds, documentation requirements, and subsidy structures mean that some individuals who previously qualified for financial assistance may face higher costs or need to re-verify eligibility. This is particularly significant for those between the ages of 60 and 64 who are not yet Medicare-eligible.

There is also a broader concern about health insurance risk pools. When immigrant populations are deterred from enrolling in marketplace plans, either due to policy changes or confusion about eligibility, the overall risk pool can shift toward sicker, higher-cost enrollees, potentially driving premiums up for everyone. This is a structural issue that affects both immigrant and non-immigrant enrollees alike.

Tricia Neuman, Senior Vice President and Executive Director of the Program on Medicare Policy at KFF, frames this in broader terms: "As the U. S. population ages and diversifies, ensuring that foreign-born residents understand their Medicare rights becomes increasingly important for health equity. " That message carries added weight as 2026 Medicare changes continue to take shape.

Marketplace Health Insurance as a Bridge for Lawfully Present Immigrants

Lawfully present immigrants in the five-year waiting period who cannot yet access Medicare or Medicaid have one primary option: ACA marketplace health insurance. Prior to recent policy changes, many in this category qualified for premium tax credits and cost-sharing reductions. Understanding what remains available in 2026 is essential.

A practical cost comparison helps frame the decision. A benchmark silver plan on the marketplace averages roughly $500–$600/month without subsidies for a 63-year-old, depending on location. Compare that to buying into Medicare Part A ($505/month maximum) plus Part B ($185/month) a combined $690/month for full Medicare access once you cross the eligibility threshold. For someone just one year away from eligibility, marketplace coverage may actually be the more cost-effective bridge.

DACA recipients and certain other non-citizen categories face continued marketplace restrictions under 2026 rules. Entering your immigration status accurately on Healthcare. gov or your state-based exchange is critical, incorrect entries can delay or invalidate your subsidy eligibility. If you are uncertain how to classify your status, a SHIP counselor or a licensed Medicare agent can help clarify your options without any cost to you. You can find additional context on Medicare vs. Obamacare to understand how the two programs interact.

How to Enroll in Medicare as a Non-Citizen: Step-by-Step Guidance

The enrollment process for lawful permanent residents follows the same timelines as for U.S. citizens, but requires a few additional steps. Here is a straightforward checklist to follow as you approach age 65:

  1. Confirm your five-year residency: Count from the date your green card was issued, not when you first arrived.
  2. Gather immigration documents: Your green card (Form I-551), naturalization certificate, or other lawful presence documentation.
  3. Verify your work quarters: Request your Social Security earnings statement at SSA.gov to confirm how many quarters you have accumulated.
  4. Identify your Initial Enrollment Period (IEP): It opens three months before the month you turn 65 and closes three months after.
  5. Enroll in Part B on time: Even if you are paying for Part A, delaying Part B enrollment triggers a 10% premium increase for every 12-month period you were eligible but did not enroll.

If you do not have 40 work quarters, you can still voluntarily enroll and pay for Part A. For many immigrants, this is still worth doing, especially when combined with Medicaid Savings Programs that may offset the cost. Review Medicare eligibility rules carefully before making this decision.

SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program) counselors offer free, unbiased guidance specifically designed for situations like this. They can walk you through your immigration-specific eligibility, explain premium options, and help you avoid costly enrollment mistakes. To find a local SHIP counselor, visit the SHIP National Technical Assistance Center at shiphelp.org.

Practical Resources and Next Steps for Immigrant Families in 2026

Staying informed requires knowing where to look. These are the most reliable, up-to-date resources for non-citizens navigating Medicare and related coverage:

  • CMS Medicare.gov: Official eligibility rules, enrollment tools, and plan comparison resources
  • Social Security Administration (SSA.gov): Work quarter verification and Medicare enrollment assistance
  • SHIP Locator (shiphelp.org): Free, state-specific Medicare counseling for seniors and their families
  • National Immigration Law Center (nilc.org): Plain-language guides on immigrants' access to public benefits, updated regularly
  • KFF Immigrant Health Coverage Fact Sheets: Data-driven summaries of Medicaid and Medicare eligibility by immigration status

Consulting a licensed Medicare agent or SHIP counselor is especially valuable right now given the 2026 policy environment. These consultations are no-cost and no-obligation, and they are designed to give you status-specific guidance rather than generic answers. Visit our Medicare agents near me page to connect with a local expert.

Document your residency carefully. The five-year clock and your work credit history are both verifiable through official records, but gathering prior employment history, tax returns, and immigration paperwork in advance will make the enrollment process far smoother. Understanding your options is the first step, and whether your path runs through Medicare, Medicaid, a marketplace plan, or some combination, help is available at every stage.

Frequently Asked Questions About Medicare for Non-Citizens

Can non-citizens get Medicare?

Yes. Lawfully present immigrants who meet the five-year residency requirement and have at least 40 work quarters can qualify for premium-free Part A, just like U. S. citizens. Those with fewer work quarters can still enroll by paying a monthly premium.

Eligibility depends on your immigration status and how long you have lived and worked legally in the U. S.

Do green card holders qualify for Medicare?

Lawful permanent residents can qualify for Medicare, but they must generally have held their green card and maintained continuous U.S. residence for at least five years. Standard age (65+) or disability criteria also apply. Meeting the work credit requirement unlocks premium-free Part A; otherwise, a monthly premium is required.

How long do you have to live in the U.S. to get Medicare?

Most non-citizens must be lawfully present and have lived continuously in the U.S. for at least five years before becoming eligible for Medicare. Refugees, asylees, and certain other humanitarian-status immigrants are generally exempt from this waiting period and may qualify sooner.

Can undocumented immigrants get Medicare?

No. Undocumented immigrants are not eligible for Medicare under current federal law. Emergency Medicaid may cover acute, life-threatening medical situations in most states regardless of immigration status, but it does not provide the same comprehensive coverage as Medicare or full Medicaid.

How much does Medicare cost for non-citizens?

Non-citizens without 40 work quarters may pay up to $505/month for Part A (2025 figures). Those with 30–39 quarters pay a reduced rate of $278/month. Part B costs $185/month for all eligible enrollees. Low-income individuals may qualify for Medicaid Savings Programs that help offset these premiums.

Can refugees or asylees get Medicare?

Yes. Refugees, asylees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, and certain other humanitarian-status immigrants are generally exempt from the five-year waiting period. They may qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid on an accelerated timeline. Verify your current eligibility with CMS or a SHIP counselor, as specific rules can change with new legislation.

How do 2026 policy changes affect immigrant health coverage?

Provisions under H. R. 1 and related 2026 healthcare legislation may alter Medicaid eligibility criteria and ACA marketplace subsidies for lawfully present immigrants. If you currently rely on marketplace coverage or Medicaid, reviewing your eligibility with a licensed counselor or your state Medicaid office is strongly recommended. See our

medicarefaq. com/faqs/medicare-costs-in-2026-premiums-deductibles-and-key-changes/">Medicare costs in 2026 page for updated premium and deductible figures.

author: David Haass
imageAlt: Navigating Medicare and Health Coverage Changes for Lawfully Present Immigrants in 2026
keyTakeaways: Medicare eligibility for non-citizens depends on their immigration status and length of U.S. residency.
Lawful permanent residents (green card holders) may qualify after meeting residency and work history requirements.
Undocumented immigrants are generally excluded from Medicare under current federal law.
To qualify for premium-free Part A, non-citizens typically need 40 quarters (10 years) of Medicare payroll tax contributions.
All eligible non-citizens face the same Medicare plan structure (Parts A, B, C, D) as U.S. citizens once qualified.
